drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Self-introduction / career & recent work?
drills.explanation: Card A01 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Self-introduction / career & recent work?
drills.explanation: Over-talk risk — three strengths, one recent signal, stop. Have your exact PhD status ready so "research background" can't read as evasive.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Self-introduction / career & recent work': Over-talk risk — three strengths, one recent signal, stop. Have your exact PhD status ready so "research background" can't read as evasive.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: RAG pilots (Digital Senpai) as the production-grade signal; hackathon as recency; Pita (250% ROI Yr2) and Cotomo (~3× retention) as range. Alt: lead with Safed COO for operator-heavy roles.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Why leave consulting for an in-house role?
drills.explanation: Card A02 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Why leave consulting for an in-house role?
drills.explanation: Don't blame execution teams ("failed the moment it left my hands") — take responsibility for the scoping gaps; lead with owning the telemetry/iteration loop.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Why leave consulting for an in-house role': Don't blame execution teams ("failed the moment it left my hands") — take responsibility for the scoping gaps; lead with owning the telemetry/iteration loop.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: SDTR breadth vs Safed end-to-end ownership (2.5× growth).

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Why this company and industry?
drills.explanation: Card A03 is tagged: Evidence-backed for Tripla.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Why this company and industry?
drills.explanation: Frame take-rate as a different model, not a smaller commission; have the CAC/unit-economics answer (AI lifts the hotel's own conversion) ready.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Why this company and industry': Frame take-rate as a different model, not a smaller commission; have the CAC/unit-economics answer (AI lifts the hotel's own conversion) ready.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Tripla take rate 1.42% vs ~25% OTA; re-ground per company.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Success / 90-day impact & successful traits?
drills.explanation: Card A04 is tagged: Framework-only — a plan, not a track record.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Success / 90-day impact & successful traits?
drills.explanation: State dated, proactive milestones; don't assume their team model; handle the legacy-mess and upstream-risk probes head-on.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Success / 90-day impact & successful traits': State dated, proactive milestones; don't assume their team model; handle the legacy-mess and upstream-risk probes head-on.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Forward-looking plan; the fast-POC capability is real and supports the quick-win claim.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Why a Senior PM role / criteria for your next step?
drills.explanation: Card A05 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Why a Senior PM role / criteria for your next step?
drills.explanation: Defuse overqualification: own a product outcome, not the org chart; say explicitly you won't reach into engineering's architecture.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Why an MBA after a tech/research background?
drills.explanation: Card A06 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Why an MBA after a tech/research background?
drills.explanation: Have one concrete unit-economics-killed-a-feature example ready; don't sound dismissive of research; "much of the job," not "the whole job."

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Your biggest career achievement?
drills.explanation: Card A07 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Your biggest career achievement?
drills.explanation: Define the ROI denominator inline; foreground the transferable method; don't dwell on press for an AI role.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Scale of startups you worked at, and where those ventures stand now?
drills.explanation: Card A08 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Scale of startups you worked at, and where those ventures stand now?
drills.explanation: Lead with the SDTR-vs-Safed clarification; say Safed was an operating role; have CAC, 6-month stickiness, and the exact Pita transaction structure ready.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Japanese proficiency / experience with Japanese customers?
drills.explanation: Card A09 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Japanese proficiency / experience with Japanese customers?
drills.explanation: Demonstrate, don't disclaim; have a Japanese answer ready for the inevitable switch — including how you'd persuade conservative stakeholders to move from legacy to AI.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Japanese proficiency / experience with Japanese customers': Demonstrate, don't disclaim; have a Japanese answer ready for the inevitable switch — including how you'd persuade conservative stakeholders to move from legacy to AI.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: ~10 yrs Tokyo; managed JP HQ in Japanese; Ginza stakeholder interviews in Japanese.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Do you have an engineering background / justify the business+technical blend?
drills.explanation: Card A10 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Do you have an engineering background / justify the business+technical blend?
drills.explanation: State exact PhD status; reconcile deterministic semantic-web vs stochastic LLM proactively (you bound the model with structure); drop "vibe-code."

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: How you work with and manage distributed, cross-border teams?
drills.explanation: Card A11 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: How you work with and manage distributed, cross-border teams?
drills.explanation: Lead with the concrete behavior (pre-read → decision → written log); clarify Transcom was coordination, not direct management of all centers; fix the mechanism, not the symptom.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Job-hunting status, certainty of moving, availability to join?
drills.explanation: Card A12 is tagged: Evidence-backed (situational).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Job-hunting status, certainty of moving, availability to join?
drills.explanation: Keep it clean and confident; don't name competitors or repeat framework language; stay flexible on the start-date probe.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: What you look for from the company / expectations of us?
drills.explanation: Card A13 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: What you look for from the company / expectations of us?
drills.explanation: State operational expectations, not a culture manifesto; caveat "experiment-friendly" hard for a high-stakes vertical so it doesn't sound reckless.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Would you consider a CEO/COO role in future?
drills.explanation: Card A14 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Would you consider a CEO/COO role in future?
drills.explanation: Reassure on flight-risk; reframe owner's mindset as prioritization discipline, not ambition for their seat; drop "survival and growth."

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Where can you help us most (e.g., within the CEO office)?
drills.explanation: Card A15 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Where can you help us most (e.g., within the CEO office)?
drills.explanation: Never call the CEO's ideas "messy"; define "validated" concretely; frame yourself as an internal owner, not a consultant. ---

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Ideate a new product or feature for our niche?
drills.explanation: Card B16 is tagged: Evidence-backed (real ideation); TAM is an estimate.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Ideate a new product or feature for our niche?
drills.explanation: Lead with the pain + how you'd validate it; show the TAM as assumptions; have the "why us vs Copilot/CI vendor" answer ready.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Ideate a new product or feature for our niche': Lead with the pain + how you'd validate it; show the TAM as assumptions; have the "why us vs Copilot/CI vendor" answer ready.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Genesys Debugger (Autify work-sample ideation); TAM is your estimate. Alt: Cotomo Talk-the-News for a consumer/data niche.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Walk through a past project — problem, contribution, hardest parts, diagnosis?
drills.explanation: Card B17 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Walk through a past project — problem, contribution, hardest parts, diagnosis?
drills.explanation: Define retention (low-single-digit baseline; matched-cohort lift); own attribution as directional; don't muddle in Digital Senpai.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Walk through a past project — problem, contribution, hardest parts, diagnosis': Define retention (low-single-digit baseline; matched-cohort lift); own attribution as directional; don't muddle in Digital Senpai.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Cotomo, ~3× retention (matched cohort) + ~50% longer sessions. Alt: Digital Senpai for a B2B/RAG panel.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Prioritize features for a roadmap or migration?
drills.explanation: Card B18 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Prioritize features for a roadmap or migration?
drills.explanation: Lead with one concrete prioritization + when you broke the framework; don't stack RICE/ICE/I-I-O as a textbook recital.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Prioritize features for a roadmap or migration': Lead with one concrete prioritization + when you broke the framework; don't stack RICE/ICE/I-I-O as a textbook recital.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Safed (overrode the score on a reliability/onboarding fix; +55% engagement / −40% TTM).

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Zero-to-one discovery, and how you choose a POC / who's involved?
drills.explanation: Card B19 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Zero-to-one discovery, and how you choose a POC / who's involved?
drills.explanation: Lead with a software example; answer the B2B-can't-pre-pay probe directly (LOIs / paid pilots / fake-door); keep Pita secondary.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Unique value proposition vs competitors / value we bring to customers?
drills.explanation: Card B20 is tagged: Evidence-backed for Tripla.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Unique value proposition vs competitors / value we bring to customers?
drills.explanation: Don't claim live inventory is itself the moat (OTAs have PMS APIs too) — the moat is the guest relationship + switching cost; don't over-defend OTAs.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: What should we do about [industry challenge] (e.g., travelers booking via ChatGPT)?
drills.explanation: Card B21 is tagged: Evidence-backed (prototype).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: What should we do about [industry challenge] (e.g., travelers booking via ChatGPT)?
drills.explanation: Correct the MCP-discovery misconception yourself; separate "machine-readable data" (real) from "agents auto-discover me" (not how MCP works).

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Why is [product area, e.g. Payments] important, and why interested given your AI background?
drills.explanation: Card B22 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Why is [product area, e.g. Payments] important, and why interested given your AI background?
drills.explanation: Don't muddle back-office forecasting with checkout conversion; state Moneytree was consulting. ---

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: How do you QA/test a non-deterministic chatbot or AI output?
drills.explanation: Card C23 is tagged: Evidence-backed (pilot-scale, not hyperscale MLOps).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: How do you QA/test a non-deterministic chatbot or AI output?
drills.explanation: Lead with "much of it is deterministic" to defuse the CI-bottleneck probe; calibrate the judge against human labels; state pilot-scale maturity.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'How do you QA/test a non-deterministic chatbot or AI output': Lead with "much of it is deterministic" to defuse the CI-bottleneck probe; calibrate the judge against human labels; state pilot-scale maturity.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Digital Senpai eval harness (designed/built at pilot scale).

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Guardrails against hallucinations / PII leaks?
drills.explanation: Card C24 is tagged: Evidence-backed on architecture; measured red-team is a framework upgrade.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Guardrails against hallucinations / PII leaks?
drills.explanation: Name OWASP LLM Top 10; lead with access control + grounding (temperature minor); fix the async hole — high-risk = synchronous pre-emission.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Guardrails against hallucinations / PII leaks': Name OWASP LLM Top 10; lead with access control + grounding (temperature minor); fix the async hole — high-risk = synchronous pre-emission.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Deterministic PII redaction (Japanese LM agent / Digital Senpai) — architecture is real; the red-team leak-rate is what you'd gate on.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Decide an AI feature is ready to release?
drills.explanation: Card C25a is tagged: Evidence-backed on the decision; full shadow/canary mechanics are framework.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Decide an AI feature is ready to release?
drills.explanation: Give concrete thresholds; explain how you evaluate shadow without ground truth (vs baseline + eval set); acknowledge shadow's cost.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Debug non-deterministic output?
drills.explanation: Card C25b is tagged: Evidence-backed on the harness; methodical steps are the articulation.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Debug non-deterministic output?
drills.explanation: Don't imply temp-0 is deterministic; lead with measuring a failure rate to separate bug from jitter; define which drift.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: AI quality measurement across multiple languages / translated output?
drills.explanation: Card C26 is tagged: Framework-only for real-time AI translation QA.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: AI quality measurement across multiple languages / translated output?
drills.explanation: COMET needs references — split offline (COMET/golden sets) from online (reference-free: LLM-judge, back-translation, task-success). Don't claim COMET on live dialogue.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Structured data for an AI agent (RAG) — what works / doesn't?
drills.explanation: Card C27 is tagged: Evidence-backed (prototype + depth); state prototype maturity.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Structured data for an AI agent (RAG) — what works / doesn't?
drills.explanation: Lead with tool-calling for live structured data (not vector-dumping); name GraphRAG as an option for relational schemas; say "prototype."

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Biggest challenges for this AI product?
drills.explanation: Card C28 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Biggest challenges for this AI product?
drills.explanation: Give the immediate staleness mitigation (don't make it a multi-quarter platform promise); name 2–3 risks with a metric each; drop the reused conversational-agent anecdote here. ---

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Preferred style of collaborating with engineers?
drills.explanation: Card D29 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Preferred style of collaborating with engineers?
drills.explanation: Vary the phrasing; show the "challenge trade-offs" line is technical, not positional; give the concrete intake mechanism, not "filtering noise."

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Preferred style of collaborating with engineers': Vary the phrasing; show the "challenge trade-offs" line is technical, not positional; give the concrete intake mechanism, not "filtering noise."
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Safed / 30-person Vietnam dev team; single-intake backlog.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: A time you disagreed with a technical stakeholder — resolution and outcome?
drills.explanation: Card D30 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: A time you disagreed with a technical stakeholder — resolution and outcome?
drills.explanation: Don't make the CTO look shallow or do "I told you so"; the lesson is influence-via-cheap-experiment; keep the lift cohort-scoped.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'A time you disagreed with a technical stakeholder — resolution and outcome': Don't make the CTO look shallow or do "I told you so"; the lesson is influence-via-cheap-experiment; keep the lift cohort-scoped.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Cotomo CTO disagreement; cohort-scoped retention lift from Talk-the-News.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Working with engineers day-to-day when products exist and there's pressure?
drills.explanation: Card D31 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Working with engineers day-to-day when products exist and there's pressure?
drills.explanation: Translate "High-Integrity Commitments" into plain terms; pre-empt the "40% = you cut testing" probe; don't pack five frameworks in.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Prove your proposal's value to other stakeholders / PMs?
drills.explanation: Card D32 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Prove your proposal's value to other stakeholders / PMs?
drills.explanation: State Sparkle's real outcome (prototype + interest), not implied revenue; show you handled KYC/AML/custody as the reason for sequencing. ---

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Define a North Star metric and sub-metrics (and does it change by stage)?
drills.explanation: Card E33 is tagged: Evidence-backed (Safed real; Langets illustrative).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Define a North Star metric and sub-metrics (and does it change by stage)?
drills.explanation: Acknowledge guardrail metrics so "one indicator" isn't naive; show how you'd measure proficiency fast (proxy checkpoints) and avoid the vanity trap.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Define a North Star metric and sub-metrics (and does it change by stage)': Acknowledge guardrail metrics so "one indicator" isn't naive; show how you'd measure proficiency fast (proxy checkpoints) and avoid the vanity trap.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Langets (illustrative) + Safed lived North Star (active users).

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Measure the success of a newly released AI feature?
drills.explanation: Card E34 is tagged: Evidence-backed (role real).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Measure the success of a newly released AI feature?
drills.explanation: Lead with speed-only-if-quality-holds; don't say "client picks most of it = working"; mention power/interleaving.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Measure the success of a newly released AI feature': Lead with speed-only-if-quality-holds; don't say "client picks most of it = working"; mention power/interleaving.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: VISASQ Time-to-First-Match (role real); shortlist ratio illustrative.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Validate the correlation between a sub-metric and the North Star?
drills.explanation: Card E35 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Validate the correlation between a sub-metric and the North Star?
drills.explanation: Name the specific confounders you controlled for; keep the lift cohort-scoped and call it directional.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: A product decision made from data analysis?
drills.explanation: Card E36 is tagged: Evidence-backed that it was a real engagement and reported outcome; treat the +40% as directional, not a personally-run RCT.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: A product decision made from data analysis?
drills.explanation: State Moneytree was consulting; present +40% as directional; don't over-claim shipped ownership. ---

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Architecture and tools of your prototype, and how you built it?
drills.explanation: Card F37 is tagged: Evidence-backed (prototype).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Architecture and tools of your prototype, and how you built it?
drills.explanation: Lead with API-vs-generation separation; say "prototype, not production"; correct the MCP-discovery misconception and name the auth/scraping risks.

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'Architecture and tools of your prototype, and how you built it': Lead with API-vs-generation separation; say "prototype, not production"; correct the MCP-discovery misconception and name the auth/scraping risks.
drills.explanation: Best response principle: triplaNeo (working prototype; Python/Next.js/Vercel). Alt: smartyPants hackathon build.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Build it in terms of resources and time (POC vs production)?
drills.explanation: Card F38 is tagged: Evidence-backed (POC + production shipping).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Build it in terms of resources and time (POC vs production)?
drills.explanation: Call the 3–5× and 30/30/30 heuristics with assumptions; name the economic kill-gate (token cost vs margin).

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Resource planning: backend engineers vs ML/data specialists?
drills.explanation: Card F39 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Resource planning: backend engineers vs ML/data specialists?
drills.explanation: Lead with "staff by uncertainty"; have the retrieval-precision-stuck reallocation example ready.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Do you also code / how much now?
drills.explanation: Card F40 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Do you also code / how much now?
drills.explanation: Drop "vibe-code" and "call BS"; be ready to name a real bug you debugged and the prototype's modules; don't claim production-engineer competence.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: RAG / agent-orchestration experience actually delivered?
drills.explanation: Card F41 is tagged: Evidence-backed (delivered to pilot).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: RAG / agent-orchestration experience actually delivered?
drills.explanation: Define pilot scope (users/period); stress you verified accuracy held, not just speed; keep MCP out unless asked. ---

drills.prompt: Interviewer pushes on 'RAG / agent-orchestration experience actually delivered': Define pilot scope (users/period); stress you verified accuracy held, not just speed; keep MCP out unless asked. ---
drills.explanation: Best response principle: Digital Senpai (delivered to pilot; ~20% / ~65%).

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Recent AI trends you're experimenting with, and how you keep sharp?
drills.explanation: Card G42 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Recent AI trends you're experimenting with, and how you keep sharp?
drills.explanation: Lead with capabilities, not framework/protocol names; explain test-time compute as a cheap-classifier latency decision; don't re-cite the hackathon as your only proof.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: In the age of AI, how should the PM role change / qualities of a great AI PM?
drills.explanation: Card G43 is tagged: Perspective.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: In the age of AI, how should the PM role change / qualities of a great AI PM?
drills.explanation: Lead with "evaluation-first" as the concrete differentiator; answer the PRD/SLA reality (error rates + guardrails, not fixed specs); keep it concrete, don't lecture.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: How you structure the AI strategy and team?
drills.explanation: Card G44 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: How you structure the AI strategy and team?
drills.explanation: Lead by solving the bottleneck (platform as self-serve API); add portfolio + cost-allocation; don't sound top-down-centralized. ---

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: How long should an efficient meeting be?
drills.explanation: Card H45 is tagged: Perspective.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: How long should an efficient meeting be?
drills.explanation: Don't sound abrasive about meetings; give the force-a-decision mechanism and the Japanese-exec nuance.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: What you learned in the Duke DeFi course / DeFi-Web3 motivation?
drills.explanation: Card H46 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: What you learned in the Duke DeFi course / DeFi-Web3 motivation?
drills.explanation: Don't overclaim "web3 is how agents will settle"; add the regulated-payments caveat; label it a hackathon; gate relevance to FinTech/Web3 roles. ---

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Is AI even the right tool — scoping an AI problem / AI vs a heuristic?
drills.explanation: Card I47 is tagged: Evidence-backed (real "don't over-engineer" calls).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Is AI even the right tool — scoping an AI problem / AI vs a heuristic?
drills.explanation: Drop the "Desirable/Possible/Probable" alliteration for the plain checklist; lead with the heuristic-first test; have the OCR throughput number ready.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: ML/AI system design (recommendation / search-ranking / fraud)?
drills.explanation: Card I48 is tagged: Framework-only on a hyperscale recommender; evidence-backed on the analogous matching problem.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: ML/AI system design (recommendation / search-ranking / fraud)?
drills.explanation: Lead with decision-type; say two-tower is "one pattern, not universal"; treat fraud differently (graph/anomaly/rules); name cold-start; separate design knowledge from delivery.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Foundation-model selection / build-vs-buy (cost, latency, quality)?
drills.explanation: Card I49 is tagged: Evidence-backed (the analysis is yours; the build wasn't).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Foundation-model selection / build-vs-buy (cost, latency, quality)?
drills.explanation: Say "build" usually = fine-tune/distill/self-host; give reasons beyond moat (privacy/latency/cost/control); state your advisory role on the from-scratch build; mention model portfolio/routing.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Prompt engineering vs RAG vs fine-tuning?
drills.explanation: Card I50 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Prompt engineering vs RAG vs fine-tuning?
drills.explanation: Reframe as knowledge (RAG/tools) vs behavior/format (fine-tune) vs workflow (agents); note LoRA/many-shot; add freshness handling.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Responsible AI, privacy, and compliance for B2B in Japan?
drills.explanation: Card I51 is tagged: Evidence-backed on operating-under-constraint + privacy-by-design; APPI/governance specifics are framework-led.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Responsible AI, privacy, and compliance for B2B in Japan?
drills.explanation: Name NIST AI RMF + OWASP LLM Top 10; don't conflate KYC/AML with AI governance; give PII specifics (NER+policy) and the vector-store deletion problem; explainability = attribution/logs, not SHAP.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Human-in-the-loop / graduated autonomy?
drills.explanation: Card I52 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Human-in-the-loop / graduated autonomy?
drills.explanation: Gate autonomy by reversibility/blast-radius, not just acceptance %; call it an improvement/eval-data queue (not "retraining" for RAG); name reviewer-bias/label-noise risk.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Latency & cost optimization?
drills.explanation: Card I53 is tagged: Evidence-backed on the trade-off; router/caching/infra are framework you'd apply, with deep infra owned by engineering.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Latency & cost optimization?
drills.explanation: Lead with budget-as-gate; caveat cache invalidation and router latency; don't claim to hand-tune compression/speculative decoding — frame as awareness + lean on engineering.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Agentic system design?
drills.explanation: Card I54 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Agentic system design?
drills.explanation: Replace "reflection" with verification + state machine + allowlists + rollback; note single-orchestrator is often safer; connect contract-net/blackboard to modern orchestration to turn the "dated PhD" probe into a strength.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: AI business case / ROI?
drills.explanation: Card I55 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: AI business case / ROI?
drills.explanation: Lead with unit economics (value-per-task − full cost-to-serve incl. data toil); anchor on Safed P&L, not the Pita watch ROI; name a concrete equation.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Data strategy for an AI product?
drills.explanation: Card I56 is tagged: Evidence-backed.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Data strategy for an AI product?
drills.explanation: Qualify the flywheel with feedback bias (selection/position/survivorship/model-induced); add data contracts; call it an improvement/eval-data queue. ---

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Incident response for an AI failure?
drills.explanation: Card J57 is tagged: Framework-led with a real severity-weighted-rollback anchor (no public AI incident run).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Incident response for an AI failure?
drills.explanation: Lead with classify→contain→remediate→RCA→prevent; name the fallback and the eval-set update; don't promise zero incidents.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Enterprise AI procurement / buyer trust?
drills.explanation: Card J58 is tagged: Framework-led, grounded in real regulated-B2B / Japan experience (no formal SOC2/ISO program run).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Enterprise AI procurement / buyer trust?
drills.explanation: Frame trust as a product deliverable (questionnaire, retention, audit logs, override, eval report); offer bounded liability + incident process, not an uncapped guarantee.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Model/provider operational risk, portability & Day-2 drift?
drills.explanation: Card J59 is tagged: Framework-led (sound design; not operated at scale).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Model/provider operational risk, portability & Day-2 drift?
drills.explanation: Lead with abstraction + cross-provider eval + fallback; name the silent-weight-change → re-calibrate-evals trap; review retention/training-use terms.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Data rights & consent — using customer data for training and evaluation?
drills.explanation: Card J60 is tagged: Framework-led, grounded in real PII / Japan-B2B experience.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Data rights & consent — using customer data for training and evaluation?
drills.explanation: Lead with "logs ≠ training/eval data; needs consent/contract, per-tenant in Japan"; name anonymization, opt-out, retention, prod/eval separation, synthetic fallback.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Pricing & packaging for an AI feature?
drills.explanation: Card J61 is tagged: Framework-led, grounded in real commercial/P&L experience (no specific AI-pricing case claimed).

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Pricing & packaging for an AI feature?
drills.explanation: Start from value metric + cost-to-serve; weigh usage vs seat vs outcome with the predictability/margin trade-offs; protect margin with caps; paid pilot over free beta.

drills.prompt: What confidence framing belongs with: Legacy-infrastructure migration & corporate gridlock (Japan enterprise)?
drills.explanation: Card J62 is tagged: Framework-led, grounded in real Japan-enterprise / incremental-delivery experience.

drills.prompt: What is the main risk in a weak answer to: Legacy-infrastructure migration & corporate gridlock (Japan enterprise)?
drills.explanation: Lead with strangler-pattern + access-layer (not big-bang); name the org gate (data owners / legacy-team relationship) as the real first-30-days work; sequence by value × access-friction. --- # PART 2 — The questions you ask them Pick 3–5 and lead with #1. Phrased so you can say them close to verbatim. 1. "Six months in, what would make you say this hire was clearly the right one?" — Lead with this. Surfaces the real success criteria and reads senior. 2. "What's the biggest struggle right now — generating ideas, building POCs, or shipping to production?" — Pinpoints where you add value. Best with CEO/founder or hiring manager. 3. "Where does AI sit in the strategy — a core platform or a feature — and which in-house AI bets are you most excited about?" — Tells you whether AI here is real or decorative. Best with leadership. 4. "How do customer or operator insights reach the roadmap today — what's the path from a real pain to a prioritized build?" — Probes discovery rigor without lecturing. Best with a product leader. 5. "What's your vision for the next one, three, and five years, and where's the market expansion?" — Long-horizon interest. Good with founders. 6. "In the first quarter, what would I own outright versus influence?" — Clarifies real scope and autonomy. 7. "How are decisions made when AI work is uncertain — who calls it, and how do you avoid over-investing in unproven bets?" — Shows you've shipped AI. Best with a CTO/technical panel. 8. (Late / logistics) "Does the company support AI tooling, learning, and conference attendance?" — Fine as a closing question. Ask carefully or skip. "Why did [predecessor] leave?" / "Why have so many people left recently?" read as red-flag-hunting. Soften to "How has the team evolved over the last year?" Selection by room. CEO/founder → #1 + #2 + #5. Product leader → #1 + #4 + #6. CTO/technical panel → #1 + #3 + #7. --- End of prep sheet (v2, hardened). Coverage: Part 1 categories A–J (63 cards: A–H = 46 with item 25 split = 47; Section I = 47–56; Section J = 57–62) + Part 2 (8 questions to ask). All metrics locked and defensibly framed; maturity stated in each answer; standards named only where defensible.
